A **nation** is a type of [social organization](/wiki/Social_organization "Social organization") where a collective [identity](/wiki/Identity_(social_science) "Identity (social science)")—a [national identity](/wiki/National_identity "National identity")—has emerged from a combination of shared features across a given population, such as [language](/wiki/Language "Language"), [history](/wiki/History "History"), [ethnicity](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity"), [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture"), [territory](/wiki/Territory "Territory"), or [society](/wiki/Society "Society"). Some nations are constructed around ethnicity (see [ethnic nationalism](/wiki/Ethnic_nationalism "Ethnic nationalism")) while others are bound by political [constitutions](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution") (see [civic nationalism](/wiki/Civic_nationalism "Civic nationalism")).[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEller1997-1)

A nation is generally more overtly political than an [ethnic group](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group").[[2]](#cite_note-black-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Benedict Anderson](/wiki/Benedict_Anderson "Benedict Anderson") defines a nation as "an [imagined political community](/wiki/Imagined_community "Imagined community") […] imagined because the members of even the smallest nation will never know most of their fellow-members, meet them, or even hear of them, yet in the minds of each lives the image of their communion",[[4]](#cite_note-4) while [Anthony D. Smith](/wiki/Anthony_D._Smith "Anthony D. Smith") defines nations as cultural-political communities that have become conscious of their [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy"), unity and particular interests.[[5]](#cite_note-Smith1991-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) *[Black's Law Dictionary](/wiki/Black%27s_Law_Dictionary "Black's Law Dictionary")* also defines nation as a community of people inhabiting a defined territory and organized under an independent government.[[2]](#cite_note-black-2) Thus, nation can be synonymous with [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") or [country](/wiki/Country "Country"). Indeed, according to [Thomas Hylland Eriksen](/wiki/Thomas_Hylland_Eriksen "Thomas Hylland Eriksen"), what distinguishes nations from other forms of collective identity, like ethnicity, is this very relationship with the state.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The consensus among scholars is that nations are [socially constructed](/wiki/Social_constructionism "Social constructionism"), historically contingent, organizationally flexible, and a distinctly [modern](/wiki/Modernity "Modernity") phenomenon.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Mylonas-9) Throughout history, people have had an attachment to their [kin group](/wiki/Kin_group "Kin group") and [traditions](/wiki/Tradition "Tradition"), territorial authorities and their homeland, but [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") – the belief that state and nation should align as a [nation state](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state") – did not become a prominent ideology until the end of the 18th century.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Etymology and terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and terminology")]

The English word *nation* from [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") c. 1300, *nacioun* "a race of people, large group of people with common ancestry and language," from [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *nacion* "birth (*naissance*), rank; descendants, relatives; country, homeland" (12c.) and directly from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *nationem* (nominative [*natio*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Special:Search/natio "wikt:Special:Search/natio") *(nātĭō), supine of verb nascar « to birth » ([supine](/wiki/Supine "Supine") : natum)*) "birth, origin; breed, stock, kind, species; race of people, tribe," literally "that which has been born," from *natus*, past participle of *nasci* "be born" ([Old Latin](/wiki/Old_Latin "Old Latin") *gnasci*), from [PIE](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language") root \*gene- "give birth, beget," with derivatives referring to procreation and familial and tribal groups.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In Latin, *natio* represents the children of the same birth and also a human group of same origin.[[12]](#cite_note-12) By [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero"), *natio* is used for "people".[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Nations in History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Nations in History")]

### The existence of earlier nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The existence of earlier nations")]

See also: [Nationalism in Antiquity](/wiki/Nationalism_in_Antiquity "Nationalism in Antiquity") and [Nationalism in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Nationalism_in_the_Middle_Ages "Nationalism in the Middle Ages")

The broad consensus amongst [scholars of nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism_studies "Nationalism studies") is that nations are a recent phenomenon.[[14]](#cite_note-14) However, some historians argue that their existence can be traced to the medieval period, or a minority believe even to antiquity.

[Adrian Hastings](/wiki/Adrian_Hastings "Adrian Hastings") argued that nations and nationalism are predominantly Christian phenomena, with Jews being the sole exception. He viewed them as the "true proto-nation" that provided the original model of nationhood through the foundational example of [ancient Israel](/wiki/Ancient_Israel "Ancient Israel") in the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible"), despite losing their political sovereignty for nearly two millennia. Regarding the latter, Jews maintained their cohesive national identity through collective memory, religion and sacred texts, which ultimately culminated in the emergence of [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") and the establishment of modern lsrael.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Anthony D. Smith wrote that the Jews of the late [Second Temple period](/wiki/Second_Temple_period "Second Temple period") provide "a closer approximation to the ideal type of the nation ... perhaps anywhere else in the ancient world."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Susan Reynolds](/wiki/Susan_Reynolds "Susan Reynolds") has argued that many European medieval kingdoms were nations in the modern sense, except that political participation in nationalism was available only to a limited prosperous and literate class,[[17]](#cite_note-17) while Hastings claims England's Anglo-Saxon kings mobilized mass nationalism in their struggle to repel Norse invasions. He argues that [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great"), in particular, drew on biblical language in his law code and that during his reign selected books of the Bible were translated into [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") to inspire Englishmen to fight to turn back the Norse invaders. Hastings argues for a strong renewal of English nationalism (following a hiatus after the [Norman conquest](/wiki/Norman_conquest "Norman conquest")) beginning with the [translation of the complete bible into English by the Wycliffe circle in the 1380s](/wiki/Wycliffe%27s_Bible "Wycliffe's Bible"), positing that the frequency and consistency in usage of the word nation from the early fourteenth century onward strongly suggest [English nationalism](/wiki/English_nationalism "English nationalism") and the English nation have been continuous since that time.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

However, [John Breuilly](/wiki/John_Breuilly "John Breuilly") criticizes Hastings's assumption that continued usage of a term such as 'English' means continuity in its meaning.[[19]](#cite_note-19) [Patrick J. Geary](/wiki/Patrick_J._Geary "Patrick J. Geary") agrees, arguing names were adapted to different circumstances by different powers and could convince people of continuity, even if radical discontinuity was the lived reality.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[Florin Curta](/wiki/Florin_Curta "Florin Curta") cites Medieval [Bulgarian nation](/wiki/Bulgarians "Bulgarians") as another possible example. [Danubian Bulgaria](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire") was founded in 680-681 as a continuation of [Great Bulgaria](/wiki/Old_Great_Bulgaria "Old Great Bulgaria"). After the adoption of [Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") in 864 it became one of the cultural centres of [Slavic](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") Europe. Its leading cultural position was consolidated with the invention of the [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") in its capital [Preslav](/wiki/Preslav "Preslav") on the eve of the 10th century.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Hugh Poulton argues the development of [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic") literacy in the country had the effect of preventing the assimilation of the [South Slavs](/wiki/South_Slavs "South Slavs") into neighboring cultures and stimulated the development of a distinct ethnic identity.[[22]](#cite_note-Poulton2000-22) A symbiosis was carried out between the numerically weak Bulgars and the numerous Slavic tribes in that broad area from the Danube to the north, to the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea") to the south, and from the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") to the west, to the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") to the east, who accepted the common ethnonym "*Bulgarians*".[[23]](#cite_note-23) During the 10th century the Bulgarians established a form of national identity that was far from modern nationalism but helped them to survive as a distinct entity through the centuries.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

[Anthony Kaldellis](/wiki/Anthony_Kaldellis "Anthony Kaldellis") asserts in *Hellenism in Byzantium* (2008) that what is called the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") was the Roman Empire transformed into a [nation-state](/wiki/Nation-state "Nation-state") in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages").[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Azar Gat also argues [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea") and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") were nations by the time of the [European Middle Ages](/wiki/European_Middle_Ages "European Middle Ages").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The *Bulletin de la Société de géographie* (1855), Maury (1855), Marty (1916), and Gadzekpo (2002) argue that, the [Serer](/wiki/Serer_people "Serer people") ethnoreligous group found in present-day Senegal, Gambia, and Mauritania, are a nation, and one of the oldest inhabitants of the [Senegambia region](/wiki/Senegambia_region "Senegambia region").[[27]](#cite_note-Bul_Soc-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) The Serers of the north originated from the ancient kingdom of [Takrur](/wiki/Takrur "Takrur"), now part of present-day Fouta Toro – which was part of Serer country,[[31]](#cite_note-31) who, [from the 11th century, faced religious and ethnic persecution](/wiki/Persecution_of_Serers "Persecution of Serers") from Islamic forces, resulting in the [Serer Exodus](/wiki/Takrur#Serer_Exodux "Takrur") from Takrur to the south.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The influence of [Serer religion](/wiki/Serer_religion "Serer religion"), [history](/wiki/Serer_ancient_history "Serer ancient history"), culture, and traditions, on the land has been so immense that, historian, Professor Charles Becker, in his paper "Vestiges historiques, trémoins matériels du passé clans les pays Sereer" (1993) writes:

> *Finally we should remember the important relic call Sereer in [Fouta](/wiki/Futa_Toro "Futa Toro"), but also in the former countries of the Ferlo, [Jolof](/wiki/Jolof_Empire "Jolof Empire") and [Kajoor](/wiki/Cayor "Cayor"), which marked the migration of proto-Sereer, whose imprint on the Fouta was so significant and remains in the memory of the [Halpulaareen](/wiki/Pulaar_language "Pulaar language")* [speakers of the Pulaar language in [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal") and the [Gambia](/wiki/Gambia "Gambia") such as the [Fula](/wiki/Fula_people "Fula people") and [Toucouleur people](/wiki/Toucouleur_people "Toucouleur people")].[[33]](#cite_note-33)

After a thousand years of [religious](/wiki/Religious_persecution "Religious persecution") and ethnic persecution, the Serer, "today scattered in several small states on the coast or pushed back into the woods of the interior, must be one of the oldest in [Senegambia](/wiki/Senegambia "Senegambia")."[[27]](#cite_note-Bul_Soc-27) They form the third largest ethnic group in [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal"), and form a minority in neighbouring [Gambia](/wiki/Gambia "Gambia"), [Mauritania](/wiki/Mauritania "Mauritania"), and to a lesser extent, [Guinea-Bissau](/wiki/Guinea-Bissau "Guinea-Bissau").

### Criticisms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Criticisms")]

In contrast, Geary rejects the conflation of early medieval and contemporary group identities as a myth, arguing it is a mistake to conclude continuity based on the recurrence of names. He criticizes historians for failing to recognize the differences between earlier ways of perceiving group identities and more contemporary attitudes, stating they are "trapped in the very historical process we are attempting to study".[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Similarly, [Sami Zubaida](/wiki/Sami_Zubaida "Sami Zubaida") notes that many states and empires in history ruled over ethnically diverse populations, and "shared ethnicity between ruler and ruled did not always constitute grounds for favour or mutual support". He goes on to argue ethnicity was never the primary basis of identification for the members of these multinational empires.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Paul Lawrence criticises Hastings's reading of [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede")'s *[Ecclesiastical History of the English People](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_History_of_the_English_People "Ecclesiastical History of the English People")* as evidence of an early [English national identity](/wiki/English_national_identity "English national identity"), instead observing that those writing so-called 'national' histories may have "been working with a rather different notion of 'the nation' to those writing history in the modern period". Lawrence goes on to argue that such documents do not demonstrate how ordinary people identified themselves, pointing out that, while they serve as texts in which an elite defines itself, "their significance in relation to what the majority thought and felt was likely to have been minor".[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Use of term *nationes* by medieval universities and other medieval institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Use of term nationes by medieval universities and other medieval institutions")]

Main article: [Nation (university)](/wiki/Nation_(university) "Nation (university)")

A significant early use of the term *nation*, as *natio*, occurred at [medieval universities](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university")[[37]](#cite_note-37) to describe the colleagues in a college or students, above all at the [University of Paris](/wiki/University_of_Paris "University of Paris"), who were all born within a *pays*, spoke the same language and expected to be ruled by their own familiar law. In 1383 and 1384, while studying theology at Paris, [Jean Gerson](/wiki/Jean_Gerson "Jean Gerson") was elected twice as a procurator for the French *natio*. The [University of Prague](/wiki/Charles_University_in_Prague "Charles University in Prague") adopted the division of students into *nationes*: from its opening in 1349 the *[studium generale](/wiki/Studium_generale "Studium generale")* which consisted of Bohemian, Bavarian, Saxon and Polish *nations*.

In a similar way, the *nationes* were segregated by the [Knights Hospitaller of Jerusalem](/wiki/Knights_Hospitaller "Knights Hospitaller"), who maintained at [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes "Rhodes") the hostels from which they took their name "where foreigners eat and have their places of meeting, each nation apart from the others, and a Knight has charge of each one of these hostels, and provides for the necessities of the inmates according to their religion", as the Spanish traveller [Pedro Tafur](/wiki/Pedro_Tafur "Pedro Tafur") noted in 1436.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Early modern nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early modern nations")]

See also: [Nation state](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state")

In his article, "The Mosaic Moment: An Early Modernist Critique of the Modernist Theory of Nationalism", [Philip S. Gorski](/wiki/Philip_S._Gorski "Philip S. Gorski") argues that the first modern [nation-state](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state") was the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), created by a fully modern political nationalism rooted in the model of [biblical nationalism](/w/index.php?title=Biblical_nationalism&action=edit&redlink=1 "Biblical nationalism (page does not exist)").[[39]](#cite_note-39) In a 2013 article "Biblical nationalism and the sixteenth-century states", [Diana Muir Appelbaum](/wiki/Diana_Muir "Diana Muir") expands Gorski's argument to apply to a series of new, Protestant, sixteenth-century nation states.[[40]](#cite_note-40) A similar, albeit broader, argument was made by Anthony D. Smith in his books, *Chosen Peoples: Sacred Sources of National Identity* and *Myths and Memories of the Nation*.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In her book *Nationalism: Five Roads to Modernity*, [Liah Greenfeld](/wiki/Liah_Greenfeld "Liah Greenfeld") argued that nationalism was invented in England by 1600. According to Greenfeld, England was “the first nation in the world".[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

For Smith, creating a 'world of nations' has had profound consequences for the global state system, as a nation comprises both a cultural and political identity. Therefore, he argues, "any attempt to forge a national identity is also a political action with political consequences, like the need to redraw the geopolitical map or alter the composition of political regimes and states".[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Social science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Social science")]

There are three notable perspectives on how nations developed. [Primordialism](/wiki/Primordialism "Primordialism") (perennialism), which reflects popular conceptions of nationalism but has largely fallen out of favour among academics,[[46]](#cite_note-46) proposes that there have always been nations and that nationalism is a natural phenomenon. [Ethnosymbolism](/wiki/Ethnosymbolism "Ethnosymbolism") explains nationalism as a dynamic, evolving phenomenon and stresses the importance of symbols, myths and traditions in the development of nations and nationalism. [Modernization theory](/wiki/Modernization_theory_(nationalism) "Modernization theory (nationalism)"), which has superseded primordialism as the dominant explanation of nationalism,[[47]](#cite_note-47) adopts a [constructivist](/wiki/Constructivism_(ethnic_politics) "Constructivism (ethnic politics)") approach and proposes that nationalism emerged due to processes of [modernization](/wiki/Modernization_theory "Modernization theory"), such as [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation"), urbanization, and mass education, which made national consciousness possible.[[9]](#cite_note-Mylonas-9)[[48]](#cite_note-Anthony_Smith-48)

Proponents of modernization theory describe nations as "[imagined communities](/wiki/Imagined_communities "Imagined communities")", a term coined by Benedict Anderson.[[49]](#cite_note-49) A nation is an imagined community in the sense that the material conditions exist for imagining extended and shared connections and that it is objectively impersonal, even if each individual in the nation experiences themselves as subjectively part of an embodied unity with others. For the most part, members of a nation remain strangers to each other and will likely never meet.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Nationalism is consequently seen an "[invented tradition](/wiki/Invented_tradition "Invented tradition")" in which shared sentiment provides a form of collective identity and binds individuals together in political solidarity. A nation's foundational "story" may be built around a combination of ethnic attributes, values and principles, and may be closely connected to narratives of belonging.[[9]](#cite_note-Mylonas-9)[[51]](#cite_note-Anderson-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Scholars in the 19th and early 20th century offered constructivist criticisms of primordial theories about nations.[[53]](#cite_note-:0-53) A prominent lecture by Ernest Renan, "[What is a Nation?](/wiki/What_Is_a_Nation%3F "What Is a Nation?")", argues that a nation is "a daily referendum", and that nations are based as much on what the people jointly forget as on what they remember. Carl Darling Buck argued in a 1916 study, "Nationality is essentially subjective, an active sentiment of unity, within a fairly extensive group, a sentiment based upon real but diverse factors, political, geographical, physical, and social, any or all of which may be present in this or that case, but no one of which must be present in all cases."[[53]](#cite_note-:0-53)

In the late 20th century, many social scientists[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] argued that there were two types of nations, the [civic nation](/wiki/Civic_nation "Civic nation") of which French republican society was the principal example and the [ethnic nation](/wiki/Ethnic_nationalism "Ethnic nationalism") exemplified by the German peoples. The German tradition was conceptualized as originating with early 19th-century philosophers, like [Johann Gottlieb Fichte](/wiki/Johann_Gottlieb_Fichte "Johann Gottlieb Fichte"), and referred to people sharing a common language, religion, culture, history, and [ethnic origins](/wiki/Ethnic_origins "Ethnic origins"), that differentiate them from people of other nations.[[54]](#cite_note-Noiriel-54) On the other hand, the civic nation was traced to the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") and ideas deriving from 18th-century French philosophers. It was understood as being centred in a willingness to "live together", this producing a nation that results from an act of affirmation.[[55]](#cite_note-55) This is the vision, among others, of [Ernest Renan](/wiki/Ernest_Renan "Ernest Renan").[[54]](#cite_note-Noiriel-54)

## Debate about a potential future of nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Debate about a potential future of nations")]

See also: [Clash of Civilizations](/wiki/Clash_of_Civilizations "Clash of Civilizations"), [Tribe (Internet)](/wiki/Tribe_(Internet) "Tribe (Internet)"), [Global citizenship](/wiki/Global_citizenship "Global citizenship"), [Geographic mobility](/wiki/Geographic_mobility "Geographic mobility"), [Transnationalism](/wiki/Transnationalism "Transnationalism"), and [Postnationalism](/wiki/Postnationalism "Postnationalism")

There is an ongoing debate about the future of nations − about whether this framework will persist as is and whether there are viable or developing alternatives.[[56]](#cite_note-news-56)

The theory of the [clash of civilizations](/wiki/Clash_of_civilizations "Clash of civilizations") lies in direct contrast to [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") theories about an ever more-connected world that no longer requires nation states. According to [political scientist](/wiki/Political_scientist "Political scientist") [Samuel P. Huntington](/wiki/Samuel_P._Huntington "Samuel P. Huntington"), people's cultural and religious [identities](/wiki/Identity_(social_science) "Identity (social science)") will be the primary source of conflict in the post–[Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") world. The theory was originally formulated in a 1992 lecture[[57]](#cite_note-57) at the [American Enterprise Institute](/wiki/American_Enterprise_Institute "American Enterprise Institute"), which was then developed in a 1993 *[Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Foreign_Affairs "Foreign Affairs")* article titled "The Clash of Civilizations?",[[58]](#cite_note-FAarticle-58) in response to [Francis Fukuyama](/wiki/Francis_Fukuyama "Francis Fukuyama")'s 1992 book, *[The End of History and the Last Man](/wiki/The_End_of_History_and_the_Last_Man "The End of History and the Last Man")*. Huntington later expanded his [thesis](/wiki/Thesis "Thesis") in a 1996 book *The Clash of Civilizations and the Remaking of World Order*.

Huntington began his thinking by surveying the diverse theories about the nature of global politics in the post–[Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") period. Some theorists and writers argued that [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights"), [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") and capitalist [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") economics had become the only remaining ideological alternative for nations in the post–Cold War world. Specifically, Francis Fukuyama, in *The End of History and the Last Man*, argued that the world had reached a [Hegelian](/wiki/Georg_Wilhelm_Friedrich_Hegel "Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel") "end of history".

Huntington believed that while the age of [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology") had ended, the world had reverted only to a normal state of affairs characterized by cultural conflict. In his thesis, he argued that the primary axis of conflict in the future will be along cultural and religious lines. [Postnationalism](/wiki/Postnationalism "Postnationalism") is the process or trend by which nation states and national identities lose their importance relative to supranational and global entities. Several factors contribute to the trend Huntington identifies, including [economic globalization](/wiki/Economic_globalization "Economic globalization"), a rise in importance of [multinational corporations](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation"), the internationalization of financial markets, the transfer of [socio-political power](/wiki/Socio-political_power "Socio-political power") from national authorities to supranational entities, such as multinational corporations, the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and the advent of new information and culture technologies such as the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"). However attachment to citizenship and [national identities](/wiki/National_identities "National identities") often remains important.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Jan Zielonka of the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") states that "the future structure and exercise of political power will resemble the medieval model more than the Westphalian one" with the latter being about "concentration of power, sovereignty and clear-cut identity" and [neo-medievalism](/wiki/Neo-medievalism "Neo-medievalism") meaning "overlapping authorities, divided sovereignty, multiple identities and governing institutions, and fuzzy borders".[[56]](#cite_note-news-56)
